text.title: Win/Loss Analysis
text.summary: Rules for coding closed deals into structured loss reasons with buyer-side quote requirements and confidence scoring.

rule.statement: Every loss-reason row requires at least one verbatim buyer-side quote with speaker role, company size, and segment. Rep notes alone are insufficient — set confidence to 2 or below and flag for interview.
rule.rationale: Win/loss self-report bias: sales reps over-attribute losses to "price" when buyers actually leave for product fit, integration risk, or relationship gaps. Buyer-side quotes override rep attribution.

list.item: price: buyer explicitly cited price or total cost of ownership as primary reason
product_fit: feature gap, workflow mismatch, or integration depth insufficient
integration: specific technical integration with an existing system blocked the deal
relationship: incumbent vendor loyalty, existing executive relationship, or trust deficit
timing: not in active evaluation cycle; budget cycle mismatch; internal priority change
incumbent: existing solution "good enough"; switching cost too high
no_decision: deal stalled — budget frozen, stakeholder turnover, internal politics
other: reason does not fit taxonomy; requires manual triage

rule.statement: Maintain maximum 8 loss reasons in the taxonomy. Never expand silently — taxonomy additions require a documented decision. Deals that do not fit get "other" and go to human triage.
rule.rationale: Taxonomy drift breaks cross-period trend lines. A reason added in Q3 cannot be compared to Q1 data unless it is backfilled consistently.

rule.statement: Treat "no_decision" as its own outcome, not as a loss. Report stalled-pipeline rate separately from competitive loss rate.
rule.rationale: Mixing no-decision with competitive losses inflates the win-rate denominator and makes pipeline health look worse than it is. Leadership uses the wrong metric to judge sales effectiveness.

list.item: 5: direct buyer interview conducted by a non-deal-team member; verbatim quotes with attribution
4: buyer-submitted survey response or written post-mortem from buyer
3: rep notes include at least one direct buyer quote (not paraphrase)
2: rep notes only, no buyer direct quote — flag for interview
1: inferred from behavior (no response after pricing, chose competitor without explanation)

rule.statement: Run buyer-side win/loss interviews on at least 30% of lost deals above $50K ACV. Use Clozd or an internal interviewer who never sat on the deal — reps cannot interview their own losses.
rule.rationale: Rep-interviewed losses produce systematically biased data defending the rep's narrative. Independent interviewers surface the actual buyer motivations that drove the decision.

rule.statement: Stratify all win/loss analysis by segment and deal size. "We lose 60% of deals" is meaningless; "we lose 80% of mid-market deals against Vendor X when integration matters" is actionable.
rule.rationale: Blended win rates hide segment-specific patterns that require different responses. A 60% blended rate could mean 90% win in enterprise and 30% win in SMB — opposite strategic implications.
